title: Database Access and Preparation
text: vignette provides tutorial accessing South Dakota State University (SDSU) bioinformatics/genomic database used Integrated Differential Expression Pathway Analysis (iDEP). also discuss leverage package prepare data analysis. current research scenarios, common work RNA-Seq counts data. data shown , hypoxia_reads, example kind data may come across naturally. data contains gene count readings glioblastoma stem-like cells derived U87MG cell line hypoxia, seen column names. species interest Human (Homo sapiens).
code: head(hypoxia_reads) #>         MRS1220_hypoxia_rep1 MRS1220_hypoxia_rep2 vehicle_hypoxia_rep1 #> A1BG                       9                   11                    4 #> A1CF                       6                    1                    4 #> A2M                        0                    0                    1 #> A2ML1                      1                    1                    0 #> A2MP1                      0                    2                    0 #> A3GALT2                    0                    0                    0 #>         vehicle_hypoxia_rep2 #> A1BG                       6 #> A1CF                       2 #> A2M                        0 #> A2ML1                      1 #> A2MP1                      0 #> A3GALT2                    1

title: Database Access and Preparation
text: considering Differential Expression Pathway Analysis methods research, may first best researcher convert gene IDs data standard Ensembl STRINGdb format. SDSU database great use case, first need know: database contain species? answer discover can identify species database, use search_species() function. search database yields following species: Human Pediculus humanus corporis Human body louse_USDA Pediculus humanus STRINGdb found species interest, Human, contained bioinformatics database labeled species ID 96. searching name_type = "" efficient, can also search primary name, academic name, ID number. However, Ensembl encoded species available entry academicName column, searching ID number requires prior knowledge database, searching “primary” name (name2 column) “” optimal results. also often primary name includes academic name, regardless. examples search methods.
code: srch_results <- search_species(   query = "Human",   name_type = "all" )  srch_results #>              ensembl_dataset #> 1      hsapiens_gene_ensembl #> 570         phumanus_eg_gene #> 2514 STRING.121224.Pediculus #>                                                                name #> 1                                            Human genes GRCh38.p14 #> 570  Pediculus humanus corporis Human body louse, USDA genes PhumU2 #> 2514                                     Pediculus humanus corporis #>                                                 name2          idType idCode #> 1                                               Human ensembl_gene_id    ens #> 570  Pediculus humanus corporis Human body louse_USDA ensembl_gene_id    ens #> 2514                       Pediculus humanus STRINGdb ensembl_gene_id    ens #>           id totalGenes          group taxon_id      academicName #> 1         96      23258        ENSEMBL     9606      homo sapiens #> 570      566      10785 EnsemblMetazoa   121224 pediculus humanus #> 2514 -121224      10761    STRINGv12.0   121224                   #>                                                                       file KEGG #> 1                                          hsapiens_gene_ensembl__Human.db  hsa #> 570  phumanus_eg_gene__Pediculus_humanus_corporis_Human_body_louse_USDA.db  phu #> 2514                         STRING.121224.Pediculus__Pediculus_humanus.db  phu #>      T.Number top #> 1      T01001   1 #> 570    T01223  NA #> 2514           NA

title: Database Access and Preparation
code: search_species(   query = "Human",   name_type = "primary" ) #>         ensembl_dataset                   name name2          idType idCode id #> 1 hsapiens_gene_ensembl Human genes GRCh38.p14 Human ensembl_gene_id    ens 96 #>   totalGenes   group taxon_id academicName                            file KEGG #> 1      23258 ENSEMBL     9606 homo sapiens hsapiens_gene_ensembl__Human.db  hsa #>   T.Number top #> 1   T01001   1

title: Database Access and Preparation
code: search_species(   query = "Homo sapiens",   name_type = "academic" ) #>      ensembl_dataset #> 570 phumanus_eg_gene #>                                                               name #> 570 Pediculus humanus corporis Human body louse, USDA genes PhumU2 #>                                                name2          idType idCode  id #> 570 Pediculus humanus corporis Human body louse_USDA ensembl_gene_id    ens 566 #>     totalGenes          group taxon_id      academicName #> 570      10785 EnsemblMetazoa   121224 pediculus humanus #>                                                                      file KEGG #> 570 phumanus_eg_gene__Pediculus_humanus_corporis_Human_body_louse_USDA.db  phu #>     T.Number top #> 570   T01223  NA

title: Database Access and Preparation
text: important piece information retain data returned search_species() id column. many functions package know species use operations.
code: search_species(   query = 96,   name_type = "id" ) #>         ensembl_dataset                   name name2          idType idCode id #> 1 hsapiens_gene_ensembl Human genes GRCh38.p14 Human ensembl_gene_id    ens 96 #>   totalGenes   group taxon_id academicName                            file KEGG #> 1      23258 ENSEMBL     9606 homo sapiens hsapiens_gene_ensembl__Human.db  hsa #>   T.Number top #> 1   T01001   1

title: Database Access and Preparation
text: Now know species ID 96, might want discover specific data metadata available species within database. list_tables() function allows us see tables present given species. identified table interest, can retrieve contents using get_table() function. instance, can fetch “geneInfo” table, contains important gene metadata like chromosomes, start positions, GC content, Ensembl IDs, . functions get_genes() get_pathways() can also used obtain gene metadata pathway information relative list genes studied. use genes hypoxia_reads data . Note functions rely Ensembl ID conversions convert_id(), discussed later vignette. retrieving pathway information, important know pathway databases (e.g. KEGG, GOBP, GOCC, etc.) available species interest. , may use path_categories(). see human species 140 different pathway databases. retrieve pathways databases inefficient. speed process using category argument get_pathways() filter specific pathway databases. argument can character vector single string. feature pathdb especially versatile, one can use vector pathway database names, e.g. c("KEGG", "GOBP", "GOCC"). way, can check databases one function call, rather pulling KEGG, GO, databases separately. Now may obtain specific set pathways relative genes hypoxia data.
code: table_names <- list_tables(species_id = 96)  table_names #> [1] "categories"  "geneInfo"    "idIndex"     "mapping"     "pathway"     #> [6] "pathwayInfo" "source" gene_info <- get_table(   species_id = 96,   table = "geneInfo" )  head(gene_info[, 1:5]) #>   ensembl_gene_id   band chromosome_name start_position percentage_gc_content #> 1 ENSG00000000003  q22.1               X      100627108                 40.40 #> 2 ENSG00000000005  q22.1               X      100584936                 40.78 #> 3 ENSG00000000419 q13.13              20       50934867                 40.20 #> 4 ENSG00000000457  q24.2               1      169849631                 40.14 #> 5 ENSG00000000460  q24.2               1      169662007                 39.22 #> 6 ENSG00000000938  p35.3               1       27612064                 52.92 gene_info <- get_genes(   species_id = 96,   genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads) )  head(gene_info[, 1:5]) #>       ensembl_gene_id   band chromosome_name start_position #> 5171  ENSG00000121410 q13.43              19       58345178 #> 9118  ENSG00000148584 q11.23              10       50799409 #> 13986 ENSG00000175899 p13.31              12             Co-expression.GTEx.Tissues.V8.2023 nrow(categories) #> [1] 140 path_info <- get_pathways(   species_id = 96,   genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),   category = c("GOBP") )  head(path_info) #>     pathwayID category #> 1      367054     GOBP #> 28     367055     GOBP #> 41     367056     GOBP #> 43     367057     GOBP #> 164    367058     GOBP #> 170    367059     GOBP #> title: Database Access and Preparation
text: identified correct species ID data (case, ID 96), can use convert_id() function standardize gene IDs. step crucial matching gene names SDSU’s database, allowing us retrieve pathway information genes proceed analysis. provide vector gene names convert_id(), return table mapping original IDs standard Ensembl format. also provide raw data frame, return data frame Ensemble gene names substituted, automatically filtering genes mapped. Please note , genes mapped Ensembl IDs, can lose large portions data. best negate , ensure IDs well documented, Ensembl counterparts, contain special characters.
code: head(rownames(hypoxia_reads))  # Providing just a vector conv_table <- convert_id(   genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),   species_id = 96 )  head(conv_table) #>        id             ens #> 1    A1BG ENSG00000121410 #> 2    A1CF ENSG00000148584 #> 3     A2M ENSG00000175899 #> 4   A2ML1 ENSG00000166535 #> 5   A2MP1 ENSG00000256069 #> 6 A3GALT2 ENSG00000184389 # Providing a vector AND data hypoxia_conv <- convert_id(   genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),   data = hypoxia_reads,   species_id = 96 )  knitr::kable(head(hypoxia_conv))

title: Database Access and Preparation
text: standardizing gene IDs, next step prior analysis typically clean count data. involves handling missing values, filtering genes consistently low expression across samples, potentially applying transformations count data, making suited exploratory differential analysis. process_data() function provides compact way complete steps analysis. can impute missing values using methods like "geneMedian" "treatAsZero", filter genes based Counts Per Million (CPM), apply normalizations base-2 log, Variance Stabilizing Transformation (VST), Regularized Log (rlog). Note given data contains column gene IDs, column stored rownames processed data. example processing converted Human data using default settings, include imputing missing values overall gene median, filtering genes least 0.5 CPM least 1 sample, returning raw counts without transformation. information covers needed properly access SDSU bioinformatics database prepare data analysis like differential expression pathway enrichment. extra filtering data need user’s end recommended developers new feature. Feel free make suggestions!
code: nrow(hypoxia_conv) #> [1] 26009  processed_data <- process_data(   data = hypoxia_conv,   missing_value = "geneMedian",   min_cpm = 0.5,   n_min_samples = 1 )  nrow(processed_data) #> [1] 12453  knitr::kable(head(processed_data[, 1:3]))

title: Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: Pathway enrichment analysis vital step understanding biological relevance differential expression results. mapping significant genes known functional categories, researchers can gain insights cellular processes pathways affected experimental conditions. vignette, explore perform pathway enrichment analysis using pathdb package conjunction popular clusterProfiler package. utilize sample dataset, hypoxia_deseq, contains differential expression results comparing treatment hypoxic conditions. First, let’s load required packages example dataset.

title: Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: performing pathway enrichment, need classify genes based expression changes. typically label genes “” regulated, “” regulated, “” significant change based fold change (log2FoldChange) adjusted p-value (padj). apply strict threshold identify significant genes, adjusted p-value less 0.05. Next, ensure gene IDs match standard format expected pathdb database, can use convert_id() function. species interest human (Species ID: 96). facilitate enrichment analysis - -regulated genes separately, split data frame list.
code: # Define up- and down- regulation based on LFC and padj df <- hypoxia_deseq |>   as.data.frame() |>   mutate(diffexp = case_when(     log2FoldChange > 0 & padj < 0.05 ~ "UP",     log2FoldChange < 0 & padj < 0.05 ~ "DOWN",     TRUE ~ "NO" # using TRUE as catch-all for padj >= 0.1 or NA   ))  # Filter out non-significant or missing adjusted p-value genes df <- df[df$diffexp != "NO" & !is.na(df$diffexp), ] # Convert ids to Ensembl format to ensure compatibility df <- convert_id(   genes = rownames(df),   data = df,   species_id = 96 ) # Split into up or down groups in a list deg_results_list <- split(df, df$diffexp)

title: Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: clusterProfiler function enricher requires background annotation data maps pathway categories (TERMs) gene IDs (GENEs). pathdb package, fetching information streamlined using T2G_prep() function. extract KEGG pathways mapping genes original hypoxia_deseq dataset, acts background universe. feature pathdb especially versatile, one can use vector pathway database names, e.g. c("KEGG", "GOBP", "GOCC"). way, can check databases one function call, rather pulling KEGG, GO, databases separately.
code: bg_genes <- T2G_prep(   species_id = 96,   category = c("KEGG"),   genes = rownames(hypoxia_deseq) )

title: Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: gene lists background annotation ready, can perform -Representation Analysis (ORA). ORA determines whether known pathways (biological functions processes) -represented (enriched) experimentally-derived gene list, lists - -regulated genes. apply enricher() function “” “” gene lists. also set cutoffs ensure results robust. performing enrichment, can format filter results. ’s often helpful combine “” “” results single data frame transform p-values easier visualization.
code: padj_cutoff <- 0.1 genecount_cutoff <- 5  # Apply enricher across Up/Down lists res <- lapply(names(deg_results_list), function(direction) {   enricher(     gene = rownames(deg_results_list[[direction]]),     pvalueCutoff = 0.05,     TERM2GENE = bg_genes   ) })  # Name the lists according to regulation names(res) <- names(deg_results_list) # Bind results by row res_df <- lapply(names(res), function(x) {   res_chunk <- res[[x]]@result   res_chunk$diffexp <- x # Keep track of the regulation direction   return(res_chunk) }) res_df <- do.call(rbind, res_df)  # Filter rows that meet our significance and count standards target_pws <- res_df$p.adjust < padj_cutoff & res_df$Count > genecount_cutoff  # Filter and select top terms res_sig <- res_df[target_pws, ] |>   arrange(p.adjust) |>   group_by(diffexp) |>   slice_head(n = 20) |> # get up to 20 top pathways per direction   ungroup()  # View enriched pathways for UP-regulated genes res_up <- res_sig |> filter(diffexp == "UP") head(res_up[, c("GeneRatio", "FoldEnrichment", "p.adjust")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   GeneRatio FoldEnrichment      p.adjust #>   <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 34/362              3.74 0.00000000203 #> 2 16/362              4.53 0.0000265     #> 3 54/362              1.98 0.0000555     #> 4 37/362              2.14 0.000479      #> 5 11/362              4.38 0.00139       #> 6 18/362              2.82 0.00237 # View enriched pathways for DOWN-regulated genes res_down <- res_sig |> filter(diffexp == "DOWN") head(res_up[, c("GeneRatio", "FoldEnrichment", "p.adjust")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   GeneRatio FoldEnrichment      p.adjust #>   <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 34/362              3.74 0.00000000203 #> 2 16/362              4.53 0.0000265     #> 3 54/362              1.98 0.0000555     #> 4 37/362              2.14 0.000479      #> 5 11/362              4.38 0.00139       #> 6 18/362              2.82 0.00237

title: Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: Unlike ORA, primarily relies artificial threshold define “significant” gene list, Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) uses genes experiment. determines whether members pathway tend occur toward top (bottom) ranked gene list. perform GSEA, must rank valid genes. One typical approach calculate metric based fold change significance level. However, simpler metric often log2FoldChange . ranked geneList, can run GSEA() function clusterProfiler. utilize background mapping (bg_genes) downloaded earlier. leveraging ORA GSEA, can obtain complementary perspectives functional changes induced experimental conditions. pathdb functions help seamlessly bridge basic count/results data advanced pathway analysis libraries.
code: # Create a ranked geneList geneList <- setNames(   object = hypoxia_deseq$log2FoldChange,   nm = rownames(hypoxia_deseq) )  # Order the list from highest to lowest geneList <- sort(geneList, decreasing = TRUE) # Execute GSEA gsea <- GSEA(   geneList = geneList,   TERM2GENE = bg_genes,   pvalueCutoff = 0.05,   maxGSSize = 2000 )  # Extract and filter top results gsea_res <- gsea@result |>   arrange(p.adjust) |>   slice(1:20)  head(gsea_res[, c("setSize", "enrichmentScore", "NES", "p.adjust")]) #>                                                      setSize enrichmentScore #> Path:hsa04110 Cell cycle                                 145      -0.6367113 #> Path:hsa04060 Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction     134       0.5991773 #> Path:hsa04657 IL-17 signaling pathway                     67       0.6490940 #> Path:hsa04640 Hematopoietic cell lineage                  37       0.7350454 #> Path:hsa05323 Rheumatoid arthritis                        52       0.6672848 #> Path:hsa03030 DNA replication                             33      -0.7249150 #>                                                            NES     p.adjust #> Path:hsa04110 Cell cycle                             -2.489013 1.665000e-08 #> Path:hsa04060 Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction  2.312135 1.665000e-08 #> Path:hsa04657 IL-17 signaling pathway                 2.244758 8.502741e-06 #> Path:hsa04640 Hematopoietic cell lineage              2.307296 3.891374e-05 #> Path:hsa05323 Rheumatoid arthritis                    2.209989 5.143430e-05 #> Path:hsa03030 DNA replication                        -2.170165 1.080804e-04

what: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis
title: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: pathdb R package designed facilitate access South Dakota State University (SDSU) bioinformatics database (used iDEP) perform essential data preparation tasks gene expression analysis. allows users seamlessly retrieve species-specific gene pathway information process RNA-Seq data downstream analysis using standard Bioconductor workflows.

title: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: can install development version pathdb GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("aidanfred24/pathdb")

title: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: Centralized Database Access: Connect directly SDSU bioinformatics database fetch comprehensive gene pathway data. Species Identification: Quickly determine organism interest supported using various search queries (common names, scientific names). ID Standardization: Convert gene identifiers standard Ensembl formats ensure compatibility differential expression pathway enrichment analysis tools. Data Pre-processing: Clean transform raw RNA-Seq count matrices (e.g., handling missing values, filtering low counts, applying VST/rlog transformations) differential expression analysis.

title: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: Instead walking complete data analysis pipeline (can find vignettes), brief look pathdb can used access genomic information species interest:
code: library(pathdb)  # 1. Check if your species is supported species_info <- search_species(query = "Human", name_type = "primary") human_id <- species_info$id[1] # ID is 96  # 2. Standardize gene IDs in your expression data data(hypoxia_reads) clean_data <- convert_id(   genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),   data = hypoxia_reads,   species_id = human_id )  # 3. Process data for downstream analysis processed_data <- process_data(   data = clean_data,   missing_value = "geneMedian",   min_cpm = 0.5,   counts_transform = 0 # 0 returns raw counts, 1=log2(CPM), 2=VST, 3=rlog )  # 4. Retrieve pathways for your genes of interest pathways <- get_pathways(   species_id = human_id,   genes = rownames(processed_data),   category = "GOBP" )

title: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: detailed, step--step tutorials fully utilize pathdb, please refer package vignettes: Database Access Preparation: vignette("data-access", package = "pathdb") Pathway Enrichment Analysis: vignette("path-enrichment", package = "pathdb")

title: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis
text: R >= 4.1.0 Imports: DBI, dplyr, edgeR, R.utils, RSQLite, stats, utils, tools

what: TERM2GENE Data Prep for iDEP Database — T2G_prep
title: TERM2GENE Data Prep for iDEP Database — T2G_prep
text: Prepares background genes enrichment analysis functions format TERM2GENE data, using pathway information various databases. Requires ID species, can filter specific vector genes.

title: TERM2GENE Data Prep for iDEP Database — T2G_prep
code: T2G_prep(species_id = NULL, category = "GOBP", genes = NULL)

title: TERM2GENE Data Prep for iDEP Database — T2G_prep
text: species_id Numeric. ID desired species database, found using srch_species() category Character. vector character constant pathway categories/databases (e.g. KEGG, GOBP, GOCC, etc.). recommended use categories, species many, leading performance issues genes Character. character vector genes add query

title: TERM2GENE Data Prep for iDEP Database — T2G_prep
text: data frame containing TERM2GENE Data (pathways genes)

title: TERM2GENE Data Prep for iDEP Database — T2G_prep
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() && curl::has_internet() # CAUTION: The human database is very large, running these examples require # the download of the human database.  # Prepare background genes mapping for Hypoxia dataset # Useful for pathway enrichment analysis of our data bg_genes <- T2G_prep(   species_id = 96,   category = "KEGG",   genes = rownames(hypoxia_deseq) ) head(bg_genes) }

title: Query Bioinformatics Database — connect_database
text: Retrieves database (.db file) SDSU bioinformatics database, creates connection via SQLite

title: Query Bioinformatics Database — connect_database
code: connect_database(species_id = NULL)

title: Query Bioinformatics Database — connect_database
text: species_id ID species selected (Loads organism info data NULL)

title: Query Bioinformatics Database — connect_database
text: SQLite connection downloaded file

title: Query Bioinformatics Database — connect_database
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() && curl::has_internet() # Connect to organism information database conn <- connect_database()  # Query information using connection x <- DBI::dbGetQuery(   conn = conn,   statement = "select * from orgInfo;"   ) head(x)  # Disconnect from database file DBI::dbDisconnect(conn = conn)  # Connect to species information database (e.g. Indian Cobra) conn <- connect_database(species_id = 99)  # Query information using connection x <- DBI::dbGetQuery(   conn = conn,   statement = "select * from geneInfo;"   ) head(x)  # Disconnect from database file DBI::dbDisconnect(conn = conn)  }

what: Convert Gene IDs to Ensembl — convert_id
title: Convert Gene IDs to Ensembl — convert_id
text: Queries database map user-provided gene identifiers Ensembl/Entrez IDs. ensure best matching conversion, please verify gene identifiers whitespace least 2 characters long.

title: Convert Gene IDs to Ensembl — convert_id
code: convert_id(genes, data = NULL, species_id, id_type = "ens")

title: Convert Gene IDs to Ensembl — convert_id
text: genes vector character string gene identifiers convert. data Optional data frame matrix. provided, function attempts match genes row names column data merges conversion results original data. species_id Numeric. ID species database connection. id_type Character. type ID convert : "ens" = Ensembl gene IDs (Default) "entrez" = Entrez gene IDs WARNING: species genes Entrez gene IDs available May take longer Ensembl IDs likely duplicate Entrez IDs

title: Convert Gene IDs to Ensembl — convert_id
text: data frame. data NULL: Returns mapping table original IDs IDs selected type. data provided: Returns data merged IDs selected type. Returns NULL species_id missing matches found. whitespace found original IDs removed.

title: Convert Gene IDs to Ensembl — convert_id
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() && curl::has_internet() # CAUTION: The human database is very large, running these examples require # the download of the human database.  # View our experimental gene IDs head(rownames(hypoxia_reads))  # Convert IDs to Ensembl format for further analysis ens_conv <- convert_id(genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),                        species_id = 96)  # Yields a conversion table for our genes head(ens_conv)  # Can also convert to Entrez IDs, if needed entrez_conv <- convert_id(genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),                           species_id = 96,                           id_type = "entrez")  # Yields a conversion table for our genes head(entrez_conv)  # We want to automatically convert our IDs within our data ens_hypoxia <- convert_id(genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),                           species_id = 96,                           data = hypoxia_reads)  # Original data head(hypoxia_reads)  # Converted data head(ens_hypoxia) }

title: Get Gene Information — get_genes
text: Retrieves gene information (e.g., Ensembl IDs, positions) specific species, optionally filtered list user-provided gene identifiers converting Ensembl IDs.

title: Get Gene Information — get_genes
code: get_genes(species_id, genes = NULL)

title: Get Gene Information — get_genes
text: species_id Numeric. ID desired species. genes vector list gene identifiers filter . NULL, returns full gene table.

title: Get Gene Information — get_genes
text: data frame containing gene information (geneInfo table). genes provided, result filtered match converted Ensembl IDs.

title: Get Gene Information — get_genes
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() && curl::has_internet() # CAUTION: The human database is very large, running these examples require # the download of the human database.  # We have gene IDs that are not commonly recognized head(rownames(hypoxia_reads))  # Retrieve gene information for genes in our sample # Converts to Ensembl IDs first genes <- get_genes(species_id = 96,                    genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads))  head(genes)  # Retrieve all genes for desired species all_genes <- get_genes(species_id = 96) head(all_genes)  # This is the same as running get_table(96, "geneInfo") all(get_genes(96) == get_table(96, "geneInfo"), na.rm = TRUE) }

title: Get Species Pathways — get_pathways
text: Retrieves pathway information specific species optionally filters specific genes.

title: Get Species Pathways — get_pathways
code: get_pathways(species_id, genes = NULL, category = "GOBP")

title: Get Species Pathways — get_pathways
text: species_id Numeric. ID desired species (e.g., srch_species). genes vector column data frame containing gene IDs interest. NULL (default), returns pathways species. category Character. vector character constant pathway categories/databases (e.g. KEGG, GOBP, GOCC, etc.). recommended use categories, species many, leading performance issues

title: Get Species Pathways — get_pathways
text: data frame containing pathway information. genes provided, data frame filtered include pathways containing genes joined gene mapping data.

title: Get Species Pathways — get_pathways
text: function first retrieves pathway pathwayInfo tables specified species. list genes provided, converts IDs Ensembl IDs, matches pathway map, joins results pathway metadata.

title: Get Species Pathways — get_pathways
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() && curl::has_internet() # CAUTION: The human database is very large, running these examples require # the download of the human database.  # Get GOBP pathways for our genes of interest path_info <- get_pathways(   species_id = 96,   genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads),   category = "GOBP" ) head(path_info) }

text: species_id Numeric. selected species ID. NULL, function defaults loading general organism info. table Character. name table retrieve (e.g., "geneInfo", "pathway"). NULL, defaults "geneInfo" (species_id provided) "orgInfo" (species_id NULL).

what: Filter Pathway Information By Category — path_filter
title: Filter Pathway Information By Category — path_filter
text: Retrieves pathway mapping information specific species, filtered one pathway categories (e.g., "GOBP", "KEGG"). Optionally, results can restricted specific list genes.

title: Filter Pathway Information By Category — path_filter
code: path_filter(species_id, genes = NULL, category = "GOBP")

title: Filter Pathway Information By Category — path_filter
text: species_id Numeric. ID species search . genes Character vector (optional). vector gene IDs filter pathways. NULL (default), pathways genes category returned. category Character character vector. pathway category categories filter (e.g., "GOBP", "KEGG", "GOCC"). Default "GOBP".

title: Filter Pathway Information By Category — path_filter
text: data frame containing pathway mapping information (gene, pathway ID, description) specified categories genes.

title: Filter Pathway Information By Category — path_filter
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() && curl::has_internet() # Get all GO Biological Process pathways for Human (ID 96) gobp_paths <- path_filter(species_id = 96, category = "GOBP")  # Get KEGG pathways for specific genes in a dataset data(hypoxia_reads) kegg_paths <- path_filter(     species_id = 96,     genes = rownames(hypoxia_reads)[1:100],     category = "KEGG" ) }

what: pathdb: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis — pathdb-package
title: pathdb: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis — pathdb-package
text: Provides access large-scale genomics data South Dakota State University's bioinformatics database, unified platform pathway analysis 13,000 organisms. includes various gene mappings, gene characteristics, pathway mapping data KEGG, GOBP, GOCC, many pathway databases. Also provides various helper functions processing RNA-Seq data differential expression analysis pathway enrichment analysis, occasionally sourced code iDEP, developed Ge, S.X., Son, E.W. & Yao, R. (2018) https://doi.org/10.1186/s12859-018-2486-6.

title: pathdb: Comprehensive Database for Pathway Enrichment Analysis — pathdb-package
text: Maintainer: Aidan Frederick aidanfred24@gmail.com contributors: Xijin Ge Xijin.Ge@sdstate.edu [funder]

title: Process Gene Expression Data — process_data
text: Performs pre-processing, missing value imputation, filtering, transformation gene expression count data.

title: Process Gene Expression Data — process_data
code: process_data(   data,   missing_value = "geneMedian",   min_cpm = 0.5,   n_min_samples = 1,   rescale = FALSE )

title: Process Gene Expression Data — process_data
text: data numeric matrix data frame (> 1 columns) gene expression counts. missing_value Character. Method handle missing values. Options: "geneMedian": Impute using median expression gene across samples. "treatAsZero": Replace NAs 0. "groupMedian": Impute using median sample group (detected colnames). min_cpm Numeric. Minimum counts per million threshold filtering genes. n_min_samples Numeric. Minimum number samples must meet min_cpm threshold gene retained. rescale Logical. TRUE allows rescaling values exceedingly large.

title: Process Gene Expression Data — process_data
text: processed transformed data matrix.

title: Process Gene Expression Data — process_data
code: # Check example data summary(pathdb::hypoxia_reads) #>  MRS1220_hypoxia_rep1 MRS1220_hypoxia_rep2 vehicle_hypoxia_rep1 #>  Min.   :     0.0     Min.   :     0.0     Min.   :     0       #>  1st Qu.:     0.0     1st Qu.:     0.0     1st Qu.:     0       #>  Median :     0.0     Median :     1.0     Median :     1       #>  Mean   :   554.5     Mean   :   554.9     Mean   :   540       #>  3rd Qu.:   128.0     3rd Qu.:   131.0     3rd Qu.:   124       #>  Max.   :295557.0     Max.   :301342.0     Max.   :328799       #>  vehicle_hypoxia_rep2 #>  Min.   :     0       #>  1st Qu.:     0       #>  Median :     0       #>  Mean   :   637       #>  3rd Qu.:   128       #>  Max.   :379017       nrow(pathdb::hypoxia_reads) #> [1] 35238  # YOU decide how your data is transformed. # Here, we want to: # Replace missing values with median # Set minimum counts-per-million of 0.4 # Meet CPM threshold in 2 samples # Keep raw counts  hypox_filtered <- process_data(data = pathdb::hypoxia_reads,                                missing_value = "geneMedian",                                min_cpm = 0.4,                                n_min_samples = 2)  # Check filtered data summary(hypox_filtered) #>  MRS1220_hypoxia_rep1 MRS1220_hypoxia_rep2 vehicle_hypoxia_rep1 #>  Min.   :     0       Min.   :     0.00    Min.   :     0       #>  1st Qu.:    70       1st Qu.:    72.25    1st Qu.:    67       #>  Median :   284       Median :   283.00    Median :   269       #>  Mean   :  1451       Mean   :  1452.09    Mean   :  1413       #>  3rd Qu.:   937       3rd Qu.:   920.00    3rd Qu.:   886       #>  Max.   :295557       Max.   :301342.00    Max.   :328799       #>  vehicle_hypoxia_rep2 #>  Min.   :     0       #>  1st Qu.:    70       #>  Median :   289       #>  Mean   :  1667       #>  3rd Qu.:  1012       #>  Max.   :379017       nrow(hypox_filtered) #> [1] 13458

what: Search for Species by Name — search_species
title: Search for Species by Name — search_species
text: Searches organism database species matching query string.

title: Search for Species by Name — search_species
code: search_species(query, name_type = "all")

title: Search for Species by Name — search_species
text: query Character. species name, partial name, ID search . name_type Character. type name search . Options: "": Default. Searches primary academic names. "academic": Scientific name. (note: available species) "primary": Primary name database (common name academic). "id": Exact species ID match.

title: Search for Species by Name — search_species
text: data frame containing information matching species. Throws error species found.

title: Search for Species by Name — search_species
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() && curl::has_internet() # Search all names for "Human" search_species(query = "Human", name_type = "all")  # Search primary names for "Human" search_species(query = "Human", name_type = "primary")  # Search academic names for "Homo sapiens" search_species(query = "Homo sapiens", name_type = "academic")  # Search by species ID search_species(query = 96, name_type = "id") }

title: pathdb 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN submission.
